sentences.text: I was talking about Yossel Davidovics.
Yossel Davidovics, we used to call him Yossel [NON-ENGLISH], because-- you asked me about nicknames.
This is one that had a nickname.
The reason why was because [NON-ENGLISH], in Yiddish, means the heavyset one, because there was another Yossel that happened to be also with the same name, but he wasn't heavyset.
So you had to distinguish between the two.
So we called him Yossel [NON-ENGLISH].
I said Yossel Davidovics.
Anyway, I remember he turned around to his son, that could have been, at that time, maybe 17, 18 years of age, and his name was Moishe Leib.
And he said to him, I told you to put my [NON-ENGLISH] bottle-- to put it in the backpack, whatever they had, and it wasn't there.
He says, and you forgot, and he says, how does a Jew travel without a [NON-ENGLISH] and without a [NON-ENGLISH]?
If this is our last journey-- this might be our last journey.
And I remember overhearing this here, and it hit me because I considered him a wise man, and that he sized up the situation.
This might be our last journey.
What it turned out, it was his last journey, and his son's, and his wife's, and his other children's.
There's two daughters survived the war.
Anyway, we got to the city of Yasinya.
I don't remember how long it took.
It must it must have taken us four or five hours, and we got unloaded there and were loaded up in Yasinya.

There was a train, there was a tunnel through the Carpathian mountain, and that was the only area where a train could go through, because that was done before World War I, yet, that the train-- so they put us on a train, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] and we arrived on the other side, which was formerly Poland, into a city the next day in the afternoon.
We arrived into a city called Kolomyja.
The Jews would call it Kolomyja.
We stayed in the train.
This was August already now, probably August the 5th.
It was a hot, hot day.
We stayed a whole day in the train.
They didn't know what to do, it seems, and [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I remember it was so hot in the train that we begged the soldiers to let us out of the train.
I got out with my brother, Tsvi, and we found a piece from a fence, a piece of lumber.
We put that down and we laid down on there.
And that was in Kolomyja.
We stayed there all night and, again, I think we could have run somewhere, but the family ties were so strong that you didn't want to.
You could have, maybe, and you didn't know what to do.

Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the next day, in the morning, we found out that there is a Hungarian Jewish labor unit working the head-- this area was already-- well, when the Germans attacked it, the Hungarians attacked that area, and it was emptied out.
The Russians, they ran.
They practically didn't put up a resistance.
It was so-- we couldn't understand it.
We thought that the mighty Soviet Union, if Germany will attack them, then it will break his neck.
But it turned out that it was a paper tiger at that time.
So we stayed there, and these soldiers, Jewish soldiers from the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], when they saw that here are whole families coming in, they didn't know.
They were out there before.
They didn't know.
Some of them found out that their own families were taken away.
So you can imagine what they felt like.
Anyway, we got unloaded again in there, and we started loading up on military trucks.
And military trucks, the same thing again happened, that people tried to stay and stick together.

And I remember distinctly that my uncle, my only-- my father's only brother, and his son, daughter, and granddaughter-- a little darling girl, three years old-- and another cousin of my father's family, they tried very hard to stick with us together on the truck, and they were terribly beaten up and put on other trucks.
And we were talking to-- we managed.
I was 21 years old.
My other brother was 33, and Moshe was still in the early 40s, so we were able to hustle up and get up on those trucks, and be together, try to keep together by force-- we forced ourselves.
And that determined our fate.
My father's brother, his family, my father's sister, her family-- that's what we saw there.
They were put up on other trucks, and we were put up-- and that determined our fate, life or death, survive or not.
Because from there, they shipped us over across the Dniester River.
The Dniester is one of the major rivers in Europe.
You have the Vistula River, and the Danube River, the Dnieper River, and the Volga river.
Those are major rivers that flow across Europe.
They put us across the Dniester river.
There was a huge, huge [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] bridge.
See my mind?
I couldn't-- the bridge was guarded by military Germans and Hungarians on both sides, because this was a major artery in the war.
The war going on already.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
Yeah, [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] we crossed the river, and we came to a place which I don't remember the name.
It was an empty field, and how many trucks-- there were two, three, four, I don't remember exactly, a few hundred of us.
They officer, Hungarian officer, that was in command of that group stopped the trucks and told us to get out of the truck and into a field, open field, a few hundred of us.
And we were set down there, and then he spoke a short sermon.
He said to us, this is your Palestine, he said.
This is where you're going to stay, and if anybody will dare to cross, go back, dare to cross the Dniester River will be shot on the spot.
And those were a very short sermon that he had, and then he ordered his police to get onto the trucks.
And they left.
It was-- and I just recall now that this was a Saturday, and I'll tell you later why I just remembered it was Saturday.
So we left on Sunday morning.
We were rounded up Saturday night to Sunday.
This is a week later.
It was on a Saturday, and it was sometime in the morning, 10, 11 o'clock in the morning, and we were on that open field.
Where do we go?
Where do we go?
What do we do?
So a whole week we managed.
We managed some.
There was no water, no food given or anything-- managed whatever we had.
We stayed there for an hour or two, I don't remember exactly.
All of a sudden, Ukrainian people came, young people, and they saw this wonder, that there are a few hundred people-- men, women, and children-- on a field.
And they introduced themselves, two guys, they introduced themselves that they are this sons of the [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], in Ukrainian, means the mayor.
Those are the mayor's sons, and we should go with them.
They will place us in certain places.
We should go with them.
So we went with them.
We didn't know where to go, so we went with them.
And the name of the village I don't remember.

I don't remember the name of the village, but those two guys, they started to separate families, and they left in-- the farmers that were there, they had huge barns, where they even were having outside where they treated their grain in the yards.
So we fell in, my brother Tsvi, and Moishe, with his children, and I, and a family that was from Budapest, a mother, a son, and a daughter.
This son was a violinist that played in the Budapest orchestra and lived there for 30 or some years.
But they, evidently, somehow they didn't have Hungarian citizenship.
Even though they lived in Hungary, the didn't have Hungarian citizenship, and they were-- somehow, how they got there, they were attached to us.
And they led us into this [NON-ENGLISH] barn.
And in that barn we had already-- there was a little straw.
We were totally exhausted from all this.
This is a week, now.
And we started asking-- we had some Hungarian money-- if we can buy something.

But the local people, they were two years under the Russian rule, and what they were missing mostly was clothing, and so they would sell something if you had-- well, we took along some underwear and some other clothing.
So if you sold some underwear you were able to get either milk, or eggs, or bread, or something.
So how do I know it was Shabbos?
That it was already a week?
We asked the people in that barn, the mayor's son, if there are Jewish people here at this village.
He said, yes, there is one Jewish family, and he is down there by the well.
You know, a local community, they had a well.
So, I picked up with my brother, Tsvi.
Moishe and the children, they stayed.
And meantime, my sister-in-law, Manci, was able to barter for some underwear or whatever she had, and got a little milk for the children and something to eat.
And Tsvi and I took off in the direction that he showed where-- so we came to a place.
We saw the local well where the people bring their water, and we got into a house.
We saw, of course, when I looked at the house I saw that there is a mezuzah, so I knew that we are in the right place.
And we came in.
We found a man slumped over a chair by the table, and he was kind of-- it was a hot day, and he was kind of taking a nap.
And when we came in we woke him up, and then I saw that there was a white tablecloth on the table.
I realized it was Shabbos.
I saw a few crumbs, and millions and millions of flies were on that table.
So we introduced ourselves, and we talked to him.
We said, are there more Jews?
He says, no, he is alone.

His daughter, he was lamenting that his daughter left with the retreating Russian army, because she was a communist official during the two years that the Russians-- from '39 to '41-- that the Russians were there.
So she was afraid to stay behind, and he was lamenting that he is alone, left all alone.
Of course, we saw that this man is of no help to us.
He can't do anything for us.
I remember just asking him, why is there so many flies here?
And he was kind of talking to himself saying, the war, the war, the war.
So the two of us walked out of there.
Hungry we were, really plenty, because whatever food we had, we didn't want to-- we kept the food for the children.
And on the fields, the fields were-- this is an area where-- it's an agrarian area.
There was a lot of corn and wheat.
It was already cut and put in bundles, but when the war broke out everything was left, and those were what the Russians call [NON-ENGLISH], so there was no direct ownership on that.
So we went out on one of those fields with my brother, Tsvi, and I, and we started taking some-- rubbing with our fingers.
We rubbed some corn, and we rubbed some of the kernels from wheat.
And we sat there for an hour or two, I don't remember exactly.
We ate some of these, and then we took up and we went back to the place where my brother Moishe was.
And of course we told them that we found that Jew and we ate already.
He said that they bought some bread.
That's why I know that it took-- we lost, in that whole tension, we lost time.
We lost it completely.
It was almost a week time.
Not almost, you know, it was Saturday.
Are we close?
We have four minutes, OK.
We stayed in that place three or four days.
In the meantime, the local people, the local Ukrainians, they were so hungry for clothing that they came.
They knew that these are-- they came.
Some had bread, eggs, cheese, and they would want to buy pants, if you had pants, or clothing.
Money they didn't want.
The Hungarian money didn't do them any good.
So we bought enough food so that we were able to survive with some bread.
And after four or five days that we were in there, the sons-- the order came that we cannot stay there anymore.
We have to go.
Where?
You cannot go back to the Dniester River.
You have to go only in one direction.
And the direction was to the next city, closer to Kamianets-Podilskyi.

It was called [NON-ENGLISH] And we got driven out from these houses-- actually barns-- and all of a sudden we found ourselves, again, a whole bunch, maybe a couple hundred of us, found ourselves on the street.
And everybody has a different opinion where are we going.
One group said, let's go to [NON-ENGLISH].
Another group said, no, let's try to get back.
We'll try to go back.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] with all this commotion we got divided up again.
One group decided to go east, and others decided-- we came into a group that decided to go back.
And the place to go back, that place was called [NON-ENGLISH].
I remember that well.
So a few hundred of us started walking toward [NON-ENGLISH], and here we had a big, big surprise.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We came into a field, and some of the people in our group were so religious that it was in the morning that they wanted to get to [INAUDIBLE] and to pray.
